text: The surgeon employs a five-port technique using the robotic system for the right adrenalectomy procedure.
The surgeon elevates the right hepatic lobe cephalad to gain access to the underlying structures during the robotic right adrenalectomy.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments, including bipolar forceps, to carefully dissect and remove adhesions between the inferior aspect of the liver and the right reticulum plutonium.
The robotic arm four uses a bowel grasper to lift the right hemiliver, demonstrating an atraumatic technique for liver manipulation.
The surgeon uses a robotic technique to take down the right coronary and triangular ligaments, similar to previous steps.
The surgeon carefully mobilizes the liver, ensuring that the right hemidiaphragm remains uninjured during the process.
The bedside assistant uses a laparoscopic suction device to evacuate smoke from the surgical field and applies posterior restriction to the kidney.
The surgeon mobilizes the right hepatic lobe, elevating it towards the lateral wall of the vena cava, revealing the anatomical relationship between the liver and the vena cava.
The vena cava is clearly visualized on the screen, with surrounding anatomical structures visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon elevates the gallbladder cephalad, preparing to resect it to improve access to the area behind the liver.
The robotic system performs a standard cholecystectomy, removing the gallbladder with precise instrument manipulation.
The surgeon transects the cystic duct after placing clips, then places the gallbladder into a laparoscopic endobag for removal from the abdomen.
The robotic arm four, equipped with a bowel grasper, is used to expose the surgical area. The right coronary and triangular ligaments are gradually taken down as the retraction progresses medially.
The surgeon encounters a lesion on the screen, indicating the start of its visualization during the procedure.
The surgeon mobilizes the liver upwards towards the midline to enhance exposure of the right adrenal gland, ensuring clear visibility of the anatomical structures.
The vena cava is dissected off the adrenal gland by removing the soft tissue attachments, with lateral traction applied using robotic bipolar forceps.
The robotic system is used to precisely dissect the soft tissue attachment with hook cautery, ensuring no thermal damage occurs to the surrounding tissues.

The surgeon dissects the Girota fascia from the lateral aspect of the tumor, revealing the capsule of the kidney posteriorly. This is accomplished using a hook cautery, ensuring good hemostasis throughout the procedure.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to dissect the adrenal gland circumferentially, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The right adrenal vein is isolated and clipped using a Hem-o-lok clip, with meticulous attention to detail during the procedure.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to carefully dissect and remove soft tissue attachments between the tumor and the lateral wall of the vena cava.

The surgeon switches from an energy device to an extended bipolar vessel sealer to achieve effective coagulation before transecting the soft tissue attachment. The vessel sealer is used to dissect the soft tissue surrounding the tumor mass, providing exposure and lateral traction to move the mass away from the vena cava as the dissection progresses cephalically.
The robotic system is used to perform a clean dissection, while the assistant evacuates smoke generated by the vessel sealer to maintain a clear operative field.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to remove the remaining attachments of the adrenal gland, achieving hemostasis in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic endobag to remove the specimen from the surgical site, similar to the technique used in laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

The surgical area is irrigated with chloropactin solution to remove minimal blood clots. The surgeon closes all port sites using direct laparoscopic visualization, ensuring no complications during closure.
